timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jeremiah Buys a Field

sections.header: Verses 1-25 -- Faith in the Midst of Siege

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah exercises his right of redemption (ge'ulla) to buy a field at Anathoth from his cousin Hanamel (v.7). The ge'ulla is the kinsman-redeemer obligation from Leviticus 25:25 -- the nearest relative must purchase family land to keep it within the clan. Buying land during a siege, when the Babylonian army surrounds the city, is an extraordinary act of faith: the purchase presupposes a future in which the land will have value.

refs.note: The kinsman-redeemer land law that Jeremiah fulfills -- keeping family property within the clan.
Boaz acts as kinsman-redeemer for Naomi's land -- the same ge'ulla institution in narrative form.
"Faith is confidence in what we hope for" -- Jeremiah's field purchase is the OT's supreme illustration.

notes.note: The transaction follows precise legal procedure: God announces the offer (v.7), Hanamel arrives (v.8), Jeremiah buys for seventeen shekels of silver (v.9). The detail validates the narrative as historical.

The deed is signed, sealed, witnessed, and stored in a clay jar for long-term preservation -- "houses, fields and vineyards will again be bought in this land" (v.15). The legal ceremony is itself a prophetic sign-act.

Jeremiah prays after the purchase, rehearsing God's mighty acts from creation to the present siege. The prayer moves from confident theology (vv.17-23) to bewildered honesty: "Yet you tell me to buy a field?" (v.25).
"Nothing is too hard for you" (lo' yippale' mimmeka kol-dabar) -- the theological foundation of the purchase. If God can create, deliver from Egypt, and bring judgment, he can restore the land.
Calvin reads the field purchase as "the supreme act of prophetic faith. Jeremiah invests in a future he will never see. This is what it means to trust God's promises against all evidence."

Calvin makes this a foundational prayer: "When we begin with God's omnipotence, every impossibility becomes a possibility. The order of faith is: first affirm what God can do, then accept what God commands."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Seventeen shekels of silver was likely a fair price for agricultural land -- suggesting that the transaction was genuinely commercial, not merely symbolic.
Storing documents in clay jars for preservation is well attested in the ancient Near East -- the Dead Sea Scrolls were preserved the same way centuries later.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies the field purchase as "the most elaborate sign-act in the book." Unlike the belt, flask, or yoke, this act costs real money and involves legal procedure. The prophet literally invests in God's promise. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls this "the most radical act of hope in the Hebrew Bible." While Babylonian siege ramps rise against the walls, the prophet signs a real estate contract. Hope is not optimism; it is the purchase of a field during a siege. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The date is 588 BC -- the tenth year of Zedekiah, the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar. Jerusalem is under siege. Jeremiah is confined in the courtyard of the guard because his prophecies of Babylonian victory were considered treason. In this setting, God commands the prophet to buy a field -- the most counterintuitive economic act imaginable. Buying real estate during a siege by the army you have prophesied will conquer is either insanity or the deepest possible faith in future restoration.

sections.header: Verses 26-44 -- "Is Anything Too Hard for Me?"

heb.paragraph: God responds to Jeremiah's prayer: "Is anything too hard (yippale') for me?" (v.27). The verb pala' means to be extraordinary, wonderful, beyond human capacity. God throws Jeremiah's own word (32:17) back at him: you affirmed that nothing is too hard for me -- now believe it.

refs.note: "Is anything too hard for the LORD?" -- God's question to Sarah uses the same root pala'. Both passages address impossibility overcome by divine power.
"Nothing will be impossible with God" -- the angel's words to Mary echo both Genesis 18 and Jeremiah 32.

notes.note: "Is anything too hard for me?" God echoes the question from Genesis 18:14, connecting the field purchase to the entire history of divine impossibilities -- from Sarah's barrenness to the restoration of exiled Israel.

The restoration promise: gather, bring back, dwell in safety, one heart, everlasting covenant, never stop doing good, plant in the land faithfully with all my heart and soul. The language is absolute.

"Just as I have brought all this great calamity, so I will give them all the prosperity I have promised." The same divine power that judged will restore. Fields will be bought again -- the purchase validated.

Calvin develops the rhetorical question: "God does not answer Jeremiah's doubts with explanation but with counter-question. The answer to 'why?' is 'can I not?' When omnipotence is affirmed, the specific 'why' becomes secondary.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "One heart and one way" (leb 'ehad wederek 'ehad) echoes Ezekiel 11:19 ("I will give them an undivided heart"). The promise is not merely political restoration but spiritual unification. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads God's response as "covenant renewal in prayer form." The prayer structure -- historical review followed by promise -- mirrors the covenant-renewal ceremony of Deuteronomy. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies five irreversible promises: gather, restore, covenant, never cease doing good, plant with all my heart. The repetition of "I will" creates a wall of divine commitment against which no counter-argument can stand. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: God's response (vv.26-44) mirrors Jeremiah's prayer structurally: a review of Israel's history (vv.28-35) followed by the promise of restoration (vv.36-44). The language is comprehensive: God will "gather them from all the lands," "bring them back to this place," give them "singleness of heart and action," make "an everlasting covenant" with them, and "never stop doing good to them" (vv.37-40). This is the new covenant of chapter 31 restated in different terms.

rows.label: vv.6-15
rows.text: The Field Purchase: Sign-Act of Hope

lit.note: The chapter pairs act (field purchase) with theology (prayer and divine response). Buying a field during a siege is the supreme expression of faith in restoration.

themes.note: Faith peaks at 10 -- the field purchase is the supreme faith act. Covenant and Sovereignty both at 9; Mercy at 8 with the everlasting covenant promise.

hebtext.note: Jeremiah exercises his right of redemption (ge'ulla) to buy a field at Anathoth from his cousin Hanamel (v.7). The ge'ulla is the kinsman-redeemer obligation from Leviticus 25:25 -- the nearest relative must purchase family land to keep it within the clan. Buying land during a siege, when the Babylonian army surrounds the city, is an extraordinary act of faith: the purchase presupposes a future in which the land will have value.

God responds to Jeremiah's prayer: "Is anything too hard (yippale') for me?" (v.27). The verb pala' means to be extraordinary, wonderful, beyond human capacity. God throws Jeremiah's own word (32:17) back at him: you affirmed that nothing is too hard for me -- now believe it.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Jeremiah Buys a Field

translations.text: Now Zedekiah king of Judah had imprisoned him there, saying, “Why do you prophesy as you do? You say, ‘This is what the Lord says: I am about to give this city into the hands of the king of Babylon, and he will capture it.
For Zedekiah king of Judah had imprisoned him, saying, “Why do you prophesy and say, ‘Thus says the LORD: Behold, I am giving this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall capture it;

“Then, just as the Lord had said, my cousin Hanamel came to me in the courtyard of the guard and said, ‘Buy my field at Anathoth in the territory of Benjamin. Since it is your right to redeem it and possess it, buy it for yourself.’ “I knew that this was the word of the Lord;

Then Hanamel my cousin came to me in the court of the guard, in accordance with the word of the LORD, and said to me, ‘Buy my field that is at Anathoth in the land of Benjamin, for the right of possession and redemption is yours; buy it for yourself.’ Then I knew that this was the word of the LORD.

thread.text: The kinsman-redeemer land law that Jeremiah fulfills -- keeping family property within the clan.
Boaz acts as kinsman-redeemer for Naomi's land -- the same ge'ulla institution in narrative form.
"Faith is confidence in what we hope for" -- Jeremiah's field purchase is the OT's supreme illustration.
"Is anything too hard for the LORD?" -- God's question to Sarah uses the same root pala'. Both passages address impossibility overcome by divine power.
"Nothing will be impossible with God" -- the angel's words to Mary echo both Genesis 18 and Jeremiah 32.

coaching.tip: Jeremiah buys a field while Jerusalem is under siege (v. 9) — the ancient equivalent of purchasing real estate during a bombing raid. The act is absurd by every human calculation and sane by theological calculation. He seals the deed and stores it because 'houses, fields, and vineyards will again be bought in this land' (v. 15). Prophetic hope isn't optimism; it's investment in God's promises against all visible evidence.

'Is anything too hard for me?' (v. 27). God's question echoes Genesis 18:14 — the same question asked about Sarah's pregnancy. The answer in both cases: the humanly impossible is divinely routine. Jeremiah's field purchase and Abraham's promised son are both acts of faith in a God who specializes in reversals. The canonical echo says: if God could give a ninety-year-old a child, he can restore a razed city.
